items.q: Why must a recursive function have a base case?
items.correct: Without it, the function calls itself indefinitely, causing a stack overflow.
items.random_wrong: To make the function run faster.
items.why: Students treat the base case as optional ceremony. It's the TERMINATION CONDITION that stops recursion - each call adds a stack frame, and without termination the stack will overflow.

items.q: How many layers are in the OSI model?
items.correct: 7 (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application).
items.why: Students confuse OSI (7 layers) with the TCP/IP model (4 layers). They are related but distinct - OSI is a theoretical reference model; TCP/IP is the practical Internet stack.

items.q: What is the difference between an IP address and a MAC address?
items.correct: MAC is a hardware identifier (fixed); IP is a logical network address (assignable).
items.misconception: They are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: IP is for input, MAC is for output.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms. MAC is burned into the network card (Layer 2); IP is assigned by the network (Layer 3). Both are needed to route packets to a device.
It's HTTPS. “HTTP” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which address is built into network hardware and rarely changes?
items.why: Correct answer: MAC address. “IP address” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: An IPv4 address is made of how many numbers separated by dots?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
